ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'iced tea' class (FOODON_03311737); no specific 'ice lemon tea' or 'homemade ice lemon tea' entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKV covers cold/iced tea as a category. No specific lemon-flavoured homemade iced tea code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for homemade ice lemon tea as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass of home-made ice lemon tea, approximately 250 ml, as typically served at a Singapore kopitiam or café.

nutrient_source.title: Lemon tea, ready-to-drink / iced lemon tea — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and HPB Singapore nutrient reference for sweetened iced tea
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) and Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'home-made ice lemon tea' exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Tea, ready-to-drink, lemon flavor, sweetened' (FDC ID 171917) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore nutrient data for sweetened iced tea. Serving size of 250 g used for GL calculation. Values represent a lightly sweetened home-made preparation typical of Singapore kopitiam style.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened iced tea and lemon tea beverages — University of Sydney GI Database reference for tea-based sweetened beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for home-made ice lemon tea. GI estimated at 38 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened iced tea and lemon-flavoured tea beverages, which report GI values in the range of 35–42 (low GI category). Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (38 × 11.5) / 100 ≈ 4.4. This is consistent with a lightly sweetened tea beverage.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~5 mg per 100 g; ~12.5 mg per 250 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Home-made ice lemon tea is very low in sodium and poses minimal concern for hypertension; however, individuals should monitor added sugar content and total fluid intake, and avoid versions with excessive sweetener or commercially added sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar content (~4.5 g sugar per 100 g; ~11.25 g per 250 ml serving) contributing to glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the glycemic index is low (estimated 38), the added sugar in a standard 250 ml serving contributes approximately 11 g of sugar and a glycemic load of ~4.4; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size or request reduced-sugar preparation to manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat, saturated fat, and cholesterol; low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Home-made ice lemon tea contains no fat, saturated fat, or cholesterol and is very low in sodium, making it a cardiovascular-friendly beverage choice when consumed without excessive added sugar; black tea polyphenols may confer modest cardioprotective benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Home-made ice lemon tea is a popular cold beverage widely consumed in Singapore, commonly served at kopitiams (traditional coffee shops), hawker centres, and cafés. It is prepared by brewing black tea (typically Ceylon or Assam), allowing it to cool, then combining it with freshly squeezed or bottled lemon juice, sugar or simple syrup, and serving over ice. The result is a refreshing, lightly sweet, and mildly tart drink with a golden-amber colour. The flavour profile balances the astringency of black tea with the bright citrus acidity of lemon and the sweetness of added sugar. Texture is thin and watery, similar to any cold tea beverage. In Singapore, ice lemon tea is a staple beverage available at virtually every food establishment and is considered a cultural comfort drink. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie beverage (~18 kcal per 100 g) composed primarily of water (~95 g per 100 g) with a modest carbohydrate content (~4.6 g per 100 g) derived almost entirely from added sugar. Protein and fat are naturally zero as this is a tea-based aqueous beverage. The glycemic index is estimated at 38 (low GI), and the glycemic load per 250 ml serving is approximately 4.4. Sodium content is very low (~5 mg per 100 g). Vitamin C is present in small amounts from lemon juice (~1.5 mg per 100 g). Black tea contributes trace amounts of potassium, iron, and antioxidant polyphenols including theaflavins and thearubigins. Clinically, this beverage is suitable for most dietary patterns when consumed with moderate sugar; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor added sugar and request reduced-sweetness versions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in ice lemon tea?
Is ice lemon tea good for diabetics?
How much sugar is in homemade ice lemon tea?

How is Singapore ice lemon tea made?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for home-made ice lemon tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from the closest USDA SR Legacy entry for sweetened ready-to-drink lemon tea (FDC ID 171917) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore data for sweetened iced tea. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened tea beverages. Confidence is medium because the food is a well-understood simple beverage with predictable composition, but the exact sugar content varies by preparation.
notes: 1. Protein_g is naturally zero as this is a tea-based aqueous beverage containing no protein-contributing ingredients. Fat_g is naturally zero as no fat-containing ingredients are used in standard home-made ice lemon tea preparation. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and dietary_fibre_g are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference for sweetened lemon tea. 2. Glycemic index set to 38 (low GI) as an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened tea beverages; no direct GI measurement for home-made ice lemon tea was found. 3. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this beverage type. Vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are -1 as these micronutrients are not reported or are below detection threshold in the reference source for this beverage. 4. The input record listed cuisine as 'Filipino' and included cuisine_tags 'Filipino, Noodle, Coffee, Cake', which are inconsistent with the food item (ice lemon tea is a quintessentially Singaporean beverage). Cuisine has been corrected to 'Singaporean' and region_of_origin set to 'Singapore'. The restaurant 'Lucky Cafe - North Canal Road' is a Singapore establishment. 5. The description in the input mentioned 'Indonesian zest', which is not a standard characteristic of home-made ice lemon tea; this has been disregarded as unsupported promotional language. 6. Price data from field survey data (SGD 1.70) is preserved in source context but not included in the nutrient record as it is not a nutritional field. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_03311737 and FoodEx2 code A0FKV are used as closest parent matches; no exact ontology code for home-made ice lemon tea exists in these systems.
